content.full_text:
FPJ-:
7ss
i
Les I ie H. Hurst
17415 Hwy 108
Jamestown, CA 95327
liu 2l
May 18, 1990
Katheri ne Donovan
Fai r Politicai Practices Conrnission
428 J. Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95814
Dear Ms Donovan:
I am writing to request a ruling on whether or not I must disqualify
myself from ruling on any projects concerning the mining company across
the street from my home.
I am the Chairman of the Jamestown Area Planning Cormission.
I have been disqualified during this past year because the mining company
purchased goods from my retail store last July that totaled more than
one tenth of one percent of our gross sales.
I understand that on July 17, when the 12 month date passes,
have a confl ict of interest from this transaction.
I wi 1l no )onger
I also live within 300 ft of the mining proiect. My husband and I receive
the 300' notices for any proiect concerning the mining company. There are
approximately 100 neighbors of the mining proiect. I understood from
Mr, Rossman ofyour office that if my property is not affected any more than
my neighbors property, I would not have a conflict of interest. However -
the question is I guess - How many neighbors? My property - and the other
property owners within the 300' feet Iine are certainly affected more than
the neighbors outside the 300'line - and yet the mining company affects a
)arge number of people in our area, probably alI the people in the jurisdiction
of my cormission.
Perhaps complicating the whole issue is the fact that I have been an
outspoken opponent o,f the mining company since 1983. I was appointed to the
Jamestown Area Planning Cormission in 1986. There have been a few small
issues concerning the mining company that have come up during the insuing years.
I have always disqualified myself (on advice of our County Council - now
deceased) because one other member of the cormission trorked for the mine and
would be disqualified - and if I also did not vote, then the people of the
area wouid be assured of an unbiased opinion.
Now that doesn't seem to be an option anynore. Four members of our 7 member
board now have conflicts of interest - and we are having to draw lots to
seat a quorum. If the 300' Iimit still causes me to have a confl ict of interest,
and my name is drawn, I understand I can only vote on the matter at hand.
However, if the 300' limit does not cause me to have a conflict, than I can
- and will - participate fully.
\(
I await your ruling!
Si ncerel y
Lesl ie H. Hurst

sections.question: I am writing to request a ruling on whether or not I must disqualify myself from ruling on any projects concerning the mining company across the street from my home. I also live within 300 ft of the mining project. My husband and I receive the 300' notices for any project concerning the mining company. There are approximately 100 neighbors of the mining project. I understood from Mr. Rossman of your office that if my property is not affected any more than my neighbors property, I would not have a conflict of interest. However - the question is I guess - How many neighbors? My property - and the other property owners within the 300' feet line are certainly affected more than the neighbors outside the 300' line - and yet the mining company affects a large number of people in our area, probably all the people in the jurisdiction of my commission.
sections.question_synthetic: As a Planning Commission Chair living within 300 feet of a mining project, must the requestor disqualify herself from voting on matters concerning that mining company, and does the 300-foot proximity threshold create a disqualifying conflict of interest when her property may be affected differently than neighbors outside that radius?
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter, not an FPPC response. No conclusion or formal Q/A sections present. The letter contains multiple related conflict of interest questions regarding business transactions (now expired), proximity-based conflicts, and bias concerns. Document appears incomplete or is awaiting FPPC response.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: I am writing to request a ruling on whether or not I must disqualify myself from ruling on any projects concerning the mining company across the street from my home. I also live within 300 ft of the mining project. My husband and I receive the 300' notices for any project concerning the mining company. There are approximately 100 neighbors of the mining project. I understood from Mr. Rossman of your office that if my property is not affected any more than my neighbors property, I would not have a conflict of interest. However - the question is I guess - How many neighbors? My property - and the other property owners within the 300' feet line are certainly affected more than the neighbors outside the 300' line - and yet the mining company affects a large number of people in our area, probably all the people in the jurisdiction of my commission.

embedding.summary: Leslie H. Hurst, Chairman of the Jamestown Area Planning Commission, requests FPPC guidance on whether she must disqualify herself from voting on matters concerning a mining company located across the street from her home, given her prior commercial transaction with the company and her residence within the 300-foot notification zone.
